output.summary: Remove the 'hero' class from PostHero's wrapper in both PHP and TSX, and create a new PostHero.css file containing the .top-gradient styles that PostHero currently inherits from Hero.css. This makes PostHero fully independent -- it will render its gradient correctly even if the Hero block is removed entirely.

context.currentDependency: PostHero.php line 28 uses class 'post-hero hero relative overflow-visible'. The 'hero' class causes PostHero to inherit .top-gradient styles from blocks/Hero/Hero.css. PostHero.tsx line 115 also includes the 'hero' class.
context.stylesInherited: Hero.css defines a single rule: .top-gradient { position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; width: 100%; height: 150vh; z-index: -10; background: 3-layer gradient using --accent-color, --color-off-white, --gradient-left, --gradient-right CSS custom properties }
context.heroInnerClass: The 'hero-inner' class has NO CSS definition anywhere -- it is purely a semantic class name used in the HTML structure. It does not need renaming.
context.jsAlreadyScoped: PostHero.js already targets '.post-hero .top-gradient' (not '.hero .top-gradient'), so removing the 'hero' class has no JS impact.
context.noCssFileExists: PostHero currently has no CSS file. One must be created.

fileChanges.description: Create PostHero's own CSS file with the .top-gradient styles currently inherited from Hero.css
Remove the 'hero' class from the wrapper section element
fileChanges.details: Line 28: Change 'post-hero hero relative overflow-visible' to 'post-hero relative overflow-visible'. Remove only the word 'hero' from the class string. All other classes remain unchanged.

fileChanges.description: Remove the 'hero' class from the editor wrapper element
fileChanges.details: Line 115: Change 'post-hero hero relative overflow-visible' to 'post-hero relative overflow-visible' in the cn() call inside useBlockProps(). Remove only the word 'hero' from the class string.

designDecisions.decision: Scope the .top-gradient selector to .post-hero .top-gradient in PostHero.css
designDecisions.evidence: Hero.css uses an unscoped .top-gradient selector. Since multiple blocks (Hero, PostHero, EventHero) all use .top-gradient child elements, scoping to .post-hero .top-gradient prevents cross-block style conflicts and ensures PostHero's CSS is truly independent.
Searched all CSS files for .hero-inner -- no CSS rule exists anywhere. The class is purely semantic/structural in the HTML and has no styling impact. Renaming it would be unnecessary churn.

designDecisions.decision: CSS custom properties (--accent-color, --color-off-white, --gradient-left, --gradient-right) remain available without the 'hero' class
designDecisions.evidence: These properties are defined in resources/css/screen/colors.css at theme level, not scoped to .hero. They are available to any element on the page.
PostHero.js line 6 targets '.post-hero .top-gradient' -- it already uses .post-hero, not .hero. Removing the 'hero' class has no JS impact.

impactAnalysis.heroBlock: No impact. Hero.css and Hero.js remain unchanged. Hero still uses its own 'hero' class.
impactAnalysis.eventHeroBlock: No impact. EventHero has its own dependency on the 'hero' class (separate issue). This change only affects PostHero.
impactAnalysis.globalCss: No impact. The .top-gradient selector in PostHero.css is scoped to .post-hero .top-gradient. The unscoped .top-gradient in Hero.css continues to work for Hero and EventHero (which still have the 'hero' class).
impactAnalysis.buildSystem: PostHero.css will be automatically picked up by the build system (webpack globs all block CSS files).

output.qaNotes: Verify: (1) PostHero gradient renders correctly on test page after removing 'hero' class, (2) Hero block gradient is unaffected, (3) PostHero.css is included in built output, (4) ResizeObserver max-height capping still works via PostHero.js
